required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of craftspeople and builders

competence_markers.description: They can describe what "done" looks like for their regular tasks — bed-making, cleanup, chores — before they begin
They complete tasks to the demonstrated standard without needing to be told what is missing
They accept redo requests without emotional escalation — redo is treated as normal, not as punishment
They put tools away properly after every use, without reminders on most occasions
They leave workspaces clean and usable after activities, without being told
They begin to self-catch quality gaps — noticing that their work does not meet the standard before the mentor points it out
They show visible satisfaction in work done well — the pride is internal, not dependent on praise
They can answer the question "Is this done, or did you stop?" honestly

narration_prompts.prompt: What does "done" look like for making your bed?
narration_prompts.detail: Describe it. Now look at your bed. Is it done?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you did something and it came out really well.
narration_prompts.detail: What was it? How did you know it was good? How did it feel?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you had to redo something.
narration_prompts.detail: What was wrong with it the first time? Was it hard to do it again? Was it better the second time?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does your workspace look like when it is clean?
narration_prompts.detail: Describe it. Could someone else use it right now, the way it is?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do we put tools away?
narration_prompts.detail: What would happen if we did not?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between "done" and "stopped"?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you give an example of each?

cross_connections.description: IC2.1 builds the structure; IC2.2 sets the quality bar within that structure. A routine done sloppily is a routine done — but IC2.2 asks: was it done well? The two work as partners.
Tool care and pride in finished work are the earliest expressions of craftsmanship. IC2.5 extends the identity of the person who does things well; IC2.2 establishes the practical habit.

The physical trades are where standards are most visibly tested. A joint that does not fit, a shelf that is not level, a seam that is crooked — these are IC2.2 failures made tangible. When the student begins trade work, the quality habits from this packet transfer directly.

Standards in creative work begin here — the habit of finishing a drawing, of noticing when the colors bleed, of caring about the result. IC2.2 prevents the "it's art, so anything goes" collapse of standards.
Technique standards in physical performance mirror quality standards in all other work. A push-up done sloppily is not a push-up. The same principle applies.
IC1.1 builds the capacity to complete tasks. IC2.2 raises the question: completed to what standard? IC1.1 says "finish." IC2.2 says "finish well."
The credibility to lead others begins with demonstrated personal standards. The child who holds themselves to quality in small things is building the foundation of leadership authority.

content.why_this_matters:
There is a difference between stopping and finishing. You know the difference already, even if no one has named it for you yet.

When you build a tower out of blocks and put every block exactly where you want it, you look at it and feel something. That feeling is pride — the real kind, the kind that comes from having done something well. Not because someone told you it was good. Because you can see it yourself. You know it is right.

Now think about the other kind of stopping. You are halfway through cleaning up your blocks, and you wander off because you saw something more interesting. The blocks are still on the floor. Some are in the bin, some are not. You did not finish. You stopped because you got tired of it, or because something else was more fun. That is not done. That is just stopped.

This packet is about learning the difference — in your body, in your hands, in your eyes — between work that is actually done and work you quit on. It is about learning to look at what you did and ask one honest question: **"Is this actually done, or did I stop because I got tired?"**

That question will follow you your whole life. The person who learns to answer it honestly, starting now, will produce work they are proud of. The person who never learns it will spend their life calling things done that are not done — and wondering why nothing they make feels right.

Standards are not about being perfect. A bed made by a five-year-old will have wrinkles. A drawing by a six-year-old will have crooked lines. That is fine. Standards are about something simpler: did you actually try? Did you do it the way you know it should be done? Or did you do the quick version because you wanted to be finished?

**"Do it right"** is a complete sentence. It means what it says. Not approximately right. Not right enough. Actually right.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The ability to see "done"** — recognizing when a task is actually complete versus when you stopped early
2. **Pride in finished work** — the internal satisfaction that comes from having done something fully and well
3. **Redo without drama** — accepting that if the work does not meet the standard, you do it again, and this is normal, not punishment
4. **Workspace standards** — leaving a space as usable as it was before you started
5. **Tool care** — putting things away properly, in the right place, in the right condition
6. **Honest self-assessment** — the beginning of the ability to look at your own work and judge whether it is actually done
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What "Done" Actually Looks Like

Before you can do something right, you need to know what right looks like. This sounds obvious, but most of the time when work is sloppy, the problem is not laziness — it is that no one showed the child what "done" actually means.

**The principle: show the standard before assigning the task.**

If the task is "make your bed," someone needs to show you what a made bed looks like. Not describe it — show it. The pillow goes here. The blanket is pulled up to here. The edges look like this. Now you know. Now when you make your bed and it does not look like that, you can see the gap yourself. You do not need someone to point it out. You already know.

If the task is "put the blocks away," someone needs to show you what "away" means. In the bin. The bin on the shelf. All of them — not most of them. Not "enough of them." All of them. That is the standard. Now you know what you are aiming at.

If the task is "wipe the table," someone needs to show you what a clean table looks like. No crumbs. No sticky spots. The cloth rinsed out and hung up. That is done. A swipe across the middle with a dry hand is not done. It is just a gesture.

**Every task has a "done" that you can see.** Learning to see it is the first skill. Learning to produce it is the second.

### Part 2: "Do It Right" — The Complete Idea

"Do it right" is one of the simplest instructions a person can receive. It is also one of the most important. It means:

- **Not approximately right.** If the shoes go on the shoe shelf, they go on the shoe shelf — not near the shoe shelf, not in the general direction of the shoe shelf.
- **Not right enough.** If there are ten blocks to put away and you put away eight, you did not put the blocks away. You put most of the blocks away. The task is not most-done. It is not done.
- **Actually right.** The standard is clear, and you met it. Not because someone was watching. Because the task has a right way, and you did it that way.

This sounds small. Shoes on a shelf. Blocks in a bin. A table wiped clean. But these small things are where the habit forms. The child who puts the shoes on the shelf — actually on the shelf, every time — is building the same internal standard they will use when they build a bookshelf at age 12, write a research paper at age 16, or deliver a report to a client at age 30.

The standard starts here. In the small things. In the things no one would notice if you did them wrong. That is exactly why they matter.

### Part 3: Tools Put Away Properly

You are not done with a task until the tools are put away. Not tossed in the general area. Put away properly.

- Crayons go back in the box. All of them. Caps on the markers.
- Scissors go in the scissor place. Not on the table. Not on the floor.
- If you used a hammer, the hammer goes back on the hook or in the toolbox.
- If you used the kitchen, the dish goes in the sink. The counter gets wiped. The chair gets pushed in.

**Why this matters:** Tools are how you do work. If you do not take care of them, they will not be there next time, or they will be damaged, or someone else will have to clean up your mess before they can do their own work. Caring for tools is respect — for the tools, for the people who share them, and for yourself.

The person who takes care of their tools is the person who can be trusted with better tools. The person who leaves them scattered is the person who is not ready for more responsibility.

### Part 4: Clean Workspaces — Leave It as You Found It

When you finish an activity, the space should look like you were never there — or better than it looked before.

This is not cleaning as punishment. This is the basic rule of shared space: **you do not leave a mess for someone else to deal with.**

- If you used the art table, the art table is clean when you are done. Not "I'll do it later." Now. Before you start the next thing.
- If you played in the living room, the living room looks the same as before you played in it.
- If you cooked with an adult, the kitchen is put back together before you eat what you made.

**The standard for a workspace is simple:** Could the next person use this space right now, without cleaning up after you first? If yes, you are done. If no, you are not done.

### Part 5: Redo Culture — When It Is Not Right, Do It Again

Sometimes you will do a task and it will not meet the standard. The bed is lumpy. The table still has crumbs. Half the crayons are missing their caps. This happens. It is not a crisis and it is not a punishment.

**The response is simple: do it again.**

Not with drama. Not with tears. Not with "but I already did it!" Yes, you did it. And it is not done. So you do it again. This is how quality works. It does not mean you are bad at this. It means the first attempt was not sufficient, and the standard has not changed just because you already tried once.

**Redo is not punishment.** This is important enough to say twice. Redo is the normal process of producing quality work. Professional writers rewrite. Professional builders remeasure. Professional cooks taste and adjust. The expectation that something should be right the first time, every time, is not a standard — it is a fantasy. The real standard is: when it is not right, you fix it. Without resentment. Without complaint. You fix it because the work matters more than your comfort.

Over time, your first attempts will get better. The redo will happen less often. But the willingness to redo — the habit of looking at your work honestly and saying "that is not right yet" — that willingness is the foundation of everything that follows.

### Part 6: Pride in Finished Tasks

There is a feeling that comes from doing something well. Not from being praised for it — from knowing, yourself, that you did it right. The table is clean. The bed is made. The tools are put away. The blocks are all in the bin. You look at it and you know: that is done.

That feeling is pride. Not the kind of pride that compares yourself to others. The kind that looks at your own work and says: "I did that. And it is good."

This feeling is the fuel for everything that comes later. The student who learns to feel it at age 5 — from a well-made bed, from a clean workspace, from a drawing they actually finished — will seek it at age 15, from a well-built project, a well-written paper, a well-executed performance. The feeling does not change. The scale does.

**You earn this feeling.** It cannot be given to you by praise. It cannot be faked by calling sloppy work "good enough." It is produced by one thing only: doing the work, doing it right, and looking at it honestly. When you earn it, it is yours. No one can take it away.
content.formative_practices:
These are not one-time activities. They are ongoing daily and weekly practices.

### Practice 1: Show the Standard [Before Every New Task]
Before the child is expected to do a task to standard, the standard must be demonstrated. Show what "done" looks like. Point to it. Have the child describe it back. Now they know the target. This is not a one-time thing — it is repeated each time a new task is introduced, and revisited when quality slips on an existing one.

### Practice 2: The Workspace Check [After Every Activity]
When the child finishes an activity, they pause and look at the space. Not a glance — an actual look. Is it clean? Are the tools put away? Could someone else use this space right now? If yes, they are done. If no, they finish. This becomes a habit — the pause-and-check before moving on.

### Practice 3: Redo Practice [As Needed]
When work does not meet the standard, the child does it again. Calmly. Without escalation. The mentor says: "This is not done yet. Here is what is missing. Do it again." The child does it again. Over time, the child begins to catch the gap themselves before the mentor does. That self-catch is the competence marker.

### Practice 4: Tool Return Ritual [After Every Use]
Every tool used is returned to its proper place before the task is declared done. This is not optional and not dependent on mood. Crayons capped and boxed. Scissors in the drawer. Hammer on the hook. Dishes in the sink. The work is not done until the tools are home.

### Practice 5: The Finished-Work Moment [Weekly]
At least once a week, the child completes something they can stand back and look at: a drawing, a chore done especially well, a model, a clean room. They pause. They look. They feel what it is like to have done it right. The mentor does not fill this moment with praise — they let the child feel it. If the mentor says anything, it is a question: "How does that look to you?"

### Practice 6: Quality Walk [Weekly]
Once a week, the child and mentor do a "quality walk" through the child's spaces — bedroom, workspace, shared areas. Together they look: Is this space at the standard? What is right? What is not? The child identifies the gaps before the mentor does, if possible. This is training the eye, not conducting an inspection.

### Practice 7: Story Discussion — Good Work [Weekly]
During read-aloud time, at least once a week, discuss a character or real person who did work carefully and well. What did they do? What would have happened if they had been sloppy? What can you tell about the kind of person they were, from the quality of their work?

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Showing the Standard

The single most common reason children produce sloppy work is that no one showed them what quality looks like. We assume children know what a clean table is, what a made bed is, what "put away properly" means. They do not. Not until someone shows them.

**Before assigning any quality-dependent task for the first time:**
1. Do the task yourself, with the child watching.
2. Point out the specific markers of "done." Not vague ("make it nice") — specific. "The pillow goes at the top. The blanket comes up to here. The edges are tucked."
3. Have the child describe what they see. "What does a made bed look like?"
4. Now they do it. And they have a reference image in their mind.

**This is not one-and-done.** The standard needs to be re-demonstrated whenever quality slips on an established task. Not as lecture — as reminder. "Let me show you again what done looks like for this."

### Holding the Standard

Showing the standard is the easy part. Holding it is where most mentors fail.

Holding the standard means:
- **When the work is not done, it is sent back.** Not with anger. Not with a sigh. "This is not done yet. Here is what is missing. Do it again."
- **The standard does not flex based on your energy level.** Tuesday's standard is the same as Saturday's. If you are too tired to enforce the standard, lower the scope of the task — assign fewer tasks — but do not lower the quality bar on the task you assigned.
- **The standard does not flex based on the child's mood.** A whiny child still wipes the table properly. A tired child still puts the blocks away. The emotional state is real and can be acknowledged, but it does not change what "done" means.
- **You inspect.** Not every task, every time — but often enough that the child knows the standard is real. The child who learns that no one ever checks learns that the standard is optional.

### The Redo Principle

Redo is the operational core of IC2.2 at Stretching Branches. It is how quality standards are actually built.

**Redo is not punishment.** This distinction matters profoundly. If redo feels like punishment — if it comes with anger, disappointment, or shaming — the child learns that falling short of the standard produces emotional pain, and they will avoid the pain by avoiding the task, hiding their work, or melting down preemptively. That is the opposite of what IC2.2 builds.

**Redo is simply the normal consequence of work that is not yet done.**

The formula:
1. Inspect the work.
2. If it meets the standard: acknowledge it simply. "That is done." (Not effusive praise for meeting a baseline.)
3. If it does not meet the standard: name what is missing. "The blocks are not all in the bin. There are three on the floor." Direct the correction. "Put those three in the bin." Wait for completion.
4. No emotional escalation. No lecture. No "how many times do I have to tell you." Just: this is not done, here is what is missing, finish it.

**Over time, the child internalizes the inspection.** They begin to look at their own work the way you have been looking at it — with honest eyes that see the gap. This is the goal. When the child catches the gap before you do, IC2.2-A is working.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Knows What "Done" Looks Like

**Developing well:** The child can describe the standard for their regular tasks before beginning. "A made bed has the blanket pulled up and the pillow at the top." They have a clear mental image of the target.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot describe what done looks like and seems surprised when told their work is incomplete. The standard has not been shown clearly enough, or not recently enough.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you actually demonstrated this task recently? Can the child see an exemplar (a clean workspace, a made bed) to reference? Is the standard age-appropriate — clear enough for a 5-year-old to visualize?

### 2. Meets the Standard Without Prompting

**Developing well:** The child produces work that meets the demonstrated standard on most occasions. Bed made properly. Tools put away. Workspace clean. Without being told what is missing.

**Developing slowly:** The child consistently produces work below the standard and requires correction every time. The gap between what was shown and what is produced is not closing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the standard clear? Is it being consistently enforced, or does it sometimes slide? Is the child getting better over time, even slowly? A consistent upward trend, however gradual, is progress.

### 3. Accepts Redo Without Drama

**Developing well:** When told to redo something, the child does it. They may not be happy about it, but they do not melt down, argue extensively, or refuse. Redo is becoming normal.

**Developing slowly:** Every redo request produces significant emotional resistance — crying, anger, "I already did it!", or refusal. The child has not yet accepted that redo is normal rather than punitive.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is your redo request delivered calmly and without emotional charge? (If you are frustrated when you ask, the child mirrors the frustration.) Is the redo request specific? ("Put those three blocks in the bin" is better than "Do it again.") Has the child been taught explicitly that redo is normal, not a punishment?

### 4. Tool Care

**Developing well:** The child returns tools to their proper place after use. Caps on markers, crayons in the box, scissors in the drawer. This is becoming automatic — they do it without being reminded.

**Developing slowly:** Tools are left wherever they were last used. The child does not connect "done with the task" with "tools put away."

**Diagnostic questions:** Does every tool have a defined "home"? (If the child does not know where the scissors go, they cannot put them there.) Is tool return included in the definition of "done" for every task? Is this being enforced — does the child get called back to put the tool away when they forget?

### 5. Workspace Standards

**Developing well:** After an activity, the child's workspace is clean and usable. Another person could use the space immediately. This is happening without prompting most of the time.

**Developing slowly:** The child leaves workspaces messy and does not connect their use of a space with its condition afterward.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the "workspace check" practice happening consistently — the pause after every activity to look at the space? Is the standard clear — does the child know what "clean enough" looks like for each space?

### 6. Self-Catching Quality Gaps

**Developing well:** The child notices their own quality gaps before you point them out. They look at the bed and say "oh, the pillow is crooked" and fix it. They scan the table after wiping and catch the spot they missed. This is the most advanced Stretching Branches marker — it indicates the standard has been internalized.

**Developing slowly:** The child never self-corrects. Every quality gap requires external identification.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you giving the child the opportunity to self-assess before you correct? Try: "Take another look. Is that done?" Give them five seconds. If they catch it, they are developing. If they always say "yes" when it clearly is not, the self-assessment muscle is not yet engaged.

### 7. Visible Satisfaction in Quality Work

**Developing well:** The child shows genuine satisfaction when they produce quality work. Not because you praised them — because they can see it themselves. They linger on a well-made bed. They stand back and look at a clean room. The feeling is earned and internal.

**Developing slowly:** The child shows no particular feeling about the quality of their work. Done is done — there is no distinction between done well and done barely.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you giving space for the child to feel this? (Rushing to the next task immediately after completion does not allow the moment.) Are you over-praising, which substitutes external validation for internal satisfaction? Try silence after good work. Let the child notice it themselves.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Praise for Incomplete Work
The most damaging thing you can do for IC2.2 development is praise work that does not meet the standard. "Good job!" for a half-wiped table teaches the child that half-wiped is the standard. They will not exceed what you accept. If the work is not done, do not call it done. If the effort was genuine but the result fell short, acknowledge the effort and direct the correction: "I can see you worked at this. The corner still has crumbs — get those, and then it is done."

### Doing It For Them
When the child produces substandard work and you redo it yourself because it is faster, you have taught two lessons: (1) their work does not actually matter, and (2) if they do it poorly enough, someone else will do it for them. Both lessons are devastating to IC2.2. If they cannot do it to standard, they do it again — not you.

### Inconsistency as the Primary Failure
Just as with IC1.1, the most common IC2.2 failure is adult inconsistency. The standard is enforced on Monday and ignored on Thursday. The bed must be made perfectly this week but "fine, whatever" next week. The child learns that standards are negotiable — that they are preferences, not principles. Once that lesson is learned, it is very difficult to unlearn.

### Perfectionism vs. Standards
IC2.2 is not perfectionism. This distinction is critical. Perfectionism says: the work is never good enough, and falling short produces anxiety and paralysis. Standards say: here is what done looks like, and you keep working until you reach it, and then you stop. The child who remakes their bed six times because it is never "perfect" is not demonstrating IC2.2 — they are demonstrating anxiety. The child who remakes their bed once because the blanket was not pulled up, and then stops because now it is done — that is IC2.2.

Watch for signs of perfectionism: excessive distress about minor imperfections, refusal to start because they might not do it perfectly, spending far more time than a task warrants. If you see these, the problem is not the standard — it is the child's relationship to falling short. Address the anxiety, not the quality bar.

### Age-Appropriate Standards
A five-year-old's "made bed" is not an adult's made bed. A six-year-old's "clean table" will not pass a restaurant inspection. The standard must be calibrated to what the child can actually produce with effort. The question is not "is this adult-quality?" but "is this what this child is capable of when they actually try?" If the answer is yes, the standard is met. If the answer is "they could have done better and they know it," the standard is not met.

### The Speed Trap
Some children rush through tasks to get to something they want to do more. The work is technically done but obviously hurried — sloppy, careless, minimum-viable. This is a standards issue, not a time-management issue. The response is not "do it faster" or "take more time" — it is "this does not meet the standard. Do it again." The child learns that rushing produces more work, not less, because the redo takes longer than doing it right the first time.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What Does "Done" Look Like?
**Strong:** Specific, visual, accurate. "The blanket is pulled up to the top. The pillow is flat and straight. No toys on the bed." The child has internalized the standard and can describe it.
**Weak:** "It looks nice" or "it looks good." Vague. The child has not internalized specific markers.

### Prompt 2: A Time It Came Out Really Well
**Strong:** Names a specific task. Describes what made it good — not "my mom said so" but observable quality markers. Shows internal satisfaction: "I could tell it was right."
**Weak:** Cannot think of an example, or the only marker of quality is external praise.

### Prompt 3: A Time You Had to Redo Something
**Strong:** Describes what was wrong the first time. Acknowledges that the redo was necessary. Notes that the second attempt was better. Does not describe the redo as punishment.
**Weak:** "I had to do it again because my mom/dad made me." The redo is framed as an imposition by the adult, not as a consequence of the work not being done.

### Prompt 4: Workspace Description
**Strong:** Specific and visual. Can describe what clean looks like. When asked about the current state, is honest about gaps.
**Weak:** "It is clean." (When it visibly is not.) The child either cannot see the gap or is not yet honest about it.

### Prompt 5: Why We Put Tools Away
**Strong:** Practical reasoning. "So they are there next time." "So they do not get broken." "So no one else has to clean up after me." Shows understanding of consequence.
**Weak:** "Because you told me to." Compliance without understanding. The rule is obeyed but not owned.

### Prompt 6: "Done" vs. "Stopped"
**Strong:** Clear distinction with examples. "Done means everything is finished and it looks right. Stopped means I quit because I did not want to do it anymore." Can give a personal example of each.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between the two, or does not understand the question.
